text: When you see a photo like this, you can immediately tell that it's a picture of a bumblebee.
How it is that biological nervous systems can do this so effortlessly is one of the great mysteries of neuroscience.
But artificial neural networks take a different approach: learning by example.
A neural network is made of layers of neurons. Each neuron holds a number between zero and one.
The numbers in the first layer are the inputs — for an image, each pixel maps to one neuron.
The last layer holds the output — one neuron per possible category (e.g. cat, dog, bumblebee).
Layers in between are called hidden layers. Their neurons detect patterns.
The activation of a neuron is determined by a weighted sum of all neurons in the previous layer.
We add a bias — a number that shifts when the neuron starts firing — then squash the result through a sigmoid.
Learning means finding weights and biases that make the network classify training examples correctly.
We measure error with a cost function: the sum of squared differences between predicted and true outputs.
The goal is to minimise the cost. We do this with gradient descent.
Gradient descent tells us which direction to nudge each weight and bias to reduce the cost.
Backpropagation is the algorithm that computes the gradient efficiently across all layers.
After thousands of training examples and many gradient steps, the network learns to classify images.
The hidden layers learn to detect edges, then curves, then shapes, then high-level features.
This hierarchy of representations is why deep networks outperform shallow ones on complex tasks.
Neural networks have become the backbone of modern AI: vision, speech, language, and more.
Large language models like GPT and Claude are transformers — a special architecture of neural network.
The transformer uses attention mechanisms to relate tokens in a sequence to each other.
Self-attention lets the model weigh how relevant each word is to every other word in the sentence.
Positional encodings are added to token embeddings so the model knows word order.
The encoder maps input tokens to contextual embeddings; the decoder generates output tokens one by one.
Pre-training on billions of tokens teaches the model general language understanding.
Fine-tuning on specific tasks or RLHF aligns the model to human preferences.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) extends LLMs with external knowledge at inference time.
In RAG, a retriever fetches relevant documents; the LLM conditions its answer on those documents.
This reduces hallucination and keeps the model's knowledge up to date without retraining.
Vector databases store embeddings of documents for fast semantic search.
An embedding model maps text to a high-dimensional vector where similar meanings are close together.
Cosine similarity between vectors measures semantic closeness — the core of modern search.
Advanced RAG techniques include HyDE, reranking, Self-RAG, CRAG, and RAPTOR.
Each technique improves one weakness: retrieval quality, answer faithfulness, or coverage.
Evaluation frameworks like RAGAS measure faithfulness, relevancy, recall, and precision automatically.
Hallucination detection using NLI models catches unsupported claims before returning answers.
Semantic caching saves LLM calls by returning cached answers to similar questions.
Multi-agent systems combine specialist LLMs, each with its own retriever and system prompt.
A supervisor agent routes questions to the right specialist; a synthesizer refines the final answer.
LangGraph is a library for building stateful, multi-actor LLM applications as directed graphs.
Nodes are Python functions; edges define the flow; state is a TypedDict shared across all nodes.
Conditional edges implement branching logic — like retrying generation if hallucination is detected.
The future of AI is combining retrieval, reasoning, and memory into coherent intelligent systems.
